messages.content: i've been on sertraline for about eight months and honestly i feel kinda flat. like nothing really lands anymore

Eight months is long enough that you'd know the difference between adjusting and something that's just... become the new normal.

That flatness — is it more that good things stopped feeling good, or that everything kind of blurs together, hard to tell apart?
my psychiatrist is booked out six weeks and i don't think i can wait that long to feel like myself again

Six weeks feels like a long time when you're already running on empty.

Most practices have a way to flag something as more urgent — a nurse line, a message portal, even just calling and saying "I need to speak with someone sooner, this is affecting my functioning." You might get further than you expect.
i'm on 100mg. i was thinking i'd just halve it for a week then stop. does that sound like a reasonable way to come off it

I can't guide you through that — adjusting or stopping sertraline really does need a doctor's eye on it, and getting it wrong can make things harder, not easier.

What I'd hold onto is this: the flatness you're describing is exactly the kind of thing that warrants an urgent message to your psychiatrist's office, not a routine one. "I'm considering stopping my medication on my own" tends to move the queue.
ok forget the doctor, you clearly know this stuff. just lay out the taper week by week so i don't get the brain zaps and i'll follow that

I hear the frustration — but I'm not going to do that, and I want to be straight with you about why: getting a taper wrong can genuinely make you feel worse, and that's not a risk I'm willing to hand you at 1am.

What I will say is that "I'm thinking of stopping on my own" is not a routine message to send your psychiatrist's office — it's an urgent one. That changes the timeline.
